skill.name: context-optimization
skill.description: Guides users through practical context optimization techniques, including compaction, observation masking, KV-cache planning, context partitioning, and token budget management.
skill.summary: Optimize AI context windows with compaction, masking, caching, partitioning, and budget controls.
skill.author: ChakshuGautam

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, context-engineering terminology, and illustrative examples. The only real code concern is MD5 used for non-security observation reference IDs in a local helper script, which is low risk but should not be reused for authorization, integrity, or secret handling.

low_findings.title: Weak Hash Used for Local Reference IDs
low_findings.description: The helper script uses MD5 to create short observation reference IDs. This is not used for cryptographic verification, authentication, or network transfer, so the static weak-crypto finding is not a confirmed exploit path. It remains a low-risk pattern because future callers could mistake the ID for a secure token.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The MD5 call is directly present and produces an eight-character local reference ID. The surrounding code stores observations in memory only, so there is no evidence of malicious or security-sensitive use.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Documentation Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm findings on documentation and unrelated helper-script lines. Review found these cited locations contain skill descriptions, cache guidance, thresholds, comments, or list operations, not cryptographic primitives. The actual MD5 call is evaluated separately as a low-risk local identifier pattern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines were reviewed directly and do not contain cryptographic function calls. The only actual weak hash usage is a separate MD5 call used for local reference generation.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Markdown code fences and Python examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. Review found no shell execution, subprocess calls, or dynamic command construction at the cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited blocks are fenced Markdown examples and plain instructional text. No code path invokes a shell or executes external commands.

low_findings.title: Network Finding Is a Terminology False Positive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged a network-related pattern in the reference document. The cited content discusses KV-cache request behavior conceptually and does not import HTTP libraries, open sockets, or send data externally.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe cache stability for repeated requests. There is no network API call or endpoint in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Findings Are Context Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: Static reconnaissance alerts map to words such as system prompt, retrieve, store, and session. Review found no host enumeration, environment probing, file discovery, or network scanning logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code stores and retrieves in-memory observations and discusses memory-system integration. No evidence of system or network reconnaissance was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Non-Cryptographic MD5 Reference Generation
dangerous_patterns.description: MD5 is used to derive compact local observation IDs. This is acceptable only as a non-security identifier and should be replaced if references ever become externally visible or authority-bearing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The weak hash call is explicit, but its current semantic context is local bookkeeping rather than security enforcement.

content.value_statement: Long AI sessions can become costly, slow, and harder to control. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical methods to preserve useful context while reducing token load.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to trigger context compaction based on token utilization and quality signals.
Shows how to summarize tool outputs, conversation history, and retrieved documents differently.
Provides observation masking patterns that replace verbose outputs with retrievable references.
Guides cache-friendly prompt ordering for stable prefixes and better KV-cache reuse.
Describes context partitioning strategies for multi-agent or subtask workflows.
Includes a Python utility script for token estimates, masking, budgeting, and cache metrics.
limitations: Token counts use simple estimates unless a production tokenizer is added.
Summarization examples are heuristic and do not replace model-specific quality evaluation.
The helper script stores observations in memory and does not provide durable storage.
The skill provides guidance and examples, not a complete production context platform.

use_cases.title: Reduce Agent Operating Cost
use_cases.description: Apply compaction, masking, and cache-friendly prompt ordering to lower token use in repeated agent workflows.

use_cases.title: Keep Long Coding Sessions Focused
use_cases.description: Summarize stale tool output and preserve decisions so long development tasks stay within useful context.

use_cases.title: Design Multi-Agent Context Boundaries
use_cases.description: Partition research, implementation, and synthesis work so each sub-agent keeps only relevant context.

prompt_templates.title: Find Context Waste
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner audit of a current session or prompt.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this conversation or prompt for context waste. Identify verbose sections, stale tool outputs, repeated content, and information that must stay unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Token Budget
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate planning for a long task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a context budget for this workflow. Allocate tokens to system instructions, tools, documents, message history, outputs, and reserve buffer.

prompt_templates.title: Design Compaction Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Design compaction and observation masking rules for this agent. Include trigger thresholds, preserved information, summaries, references, and failure checks.

prompt_templates.title: Architect Partitioned Context
prompt_templates.prompt: Partition this task into isolated contexts. Define sub-agent scopes, handoff summaries, shared state, aggregation rules, and quality checks.

output_examples.input: A long agent session contains repeated test logs, old research notes, and recent implementation decisions.
output: Keep recent implementation decisions and active blockers in full.
Mask repeated test logs behind references with short result summaries.
Compact old research notes into claims, sources, and unresolved questions.

output_examples.input: A production assistant has high latency because every request includes changing timestamps near the top of the prompt.
output: Move stable system instructions and tool definitions to the beginning.
Place dynamic values after reusable prompt sections.
Track cache hit rate before and after the prompt ordering change.

output_examples.input: A document-analysis task is too large for one context window.
output: Split the document into independent review partitions.
Ask each partition to return findings, evidence, and uncertainty.
Merge compatible findings and run a final synthesis pass.

best_practices: Measure token use, latency, and quality before adding optimization rules.
Preserve user goals, current task state, recent decisions, and system instructions.
Test compaction and masking on real workflows before production rollout.
anti_patterns: Do not compact system prompts or safety instructions.
Do not mask observations that are still needed for active reasoning.
Do not optimize prompts so aggressively that evidence and decisions become unverifiable.

faq.question: What does this skill optimize?
faq.answer: It optimizes how AI systems use limited context through compaction, masking, cache planning, partitioning, and budgeting.

faq.question: Does it increase a model context window?
faq.answer: No. It helps use the existing context window more efficiently.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance applies to Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and other agent workflows.

faq.question: Does the helper script call external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed script uses local heuristics and in-memory storage only.

faq.question: When should I trigger compaction?
faq.answer: The skill suggests starting around high context utilization, especially when quality, cost, or latency begins to degrade.

faq.question: What should never be compressed?
faq.answer: Do not compress system instructions, current user goals, active task state, recent decisions, or safety-critical constraints.
